title: Numbers and nouns
subtitle: Why the noun changes after 1, 2, and 5

vocabulary.exampleMeaning: How many books do you have?

hook.text: You know the numbers. You know the nouns. Now comes the rule that every Russian learner has to learn eventually: the noun after a number doesn't stay in the same form. один дом, два дома, пять домов — three different noun forms for three different numbers. The rule is logical once you see it.

why.text: This rule comes from the historical case system — 'two houses' was originally 'two of house' (genitive singular), and 'five houses' was 'five of houses' (genitive plural). The logic is there even if the surface forms look arbitrary. Once you understand the three zones (1 / 2-4 / 5+), you can apply the rule to any noun.

cards.title: The three counting zones
cards.body: Russian divides numbers into three zones, each requiring a different noun form. Zone 1: the number ends in 1 → nominative singular. Zone 2: the number ends in 2, 3, or 4 → genitive singular. Zone 3: the number ends in 5–20 (or 0) → genitive plural.

cards.title: The teens exception
cards.body: Numbers 11–19 all take genitive plural, even though they end in 1, 2, 3, 4. This is the one exception to the 'ends in' rule. 11 домов (not 11 дома), 14 книг (not 14 книги).

cards.title: Genitive singular — how to form it
cards.body: You need to know the genitive singular because 2, 3, 4 require it. Masculine nouns add -А/-Я. Feminine -А nouns replace А with -Ы/-И. Neuter -О/-Е nouns replace the ending with -А/-Я.

cards.title: Genitive plural — how to form it
cards.body: Genitive plural is the most complex form. For many masculine nouns: add -ОВ/-ЕВ. Feminine and neuter nouns: remove the final vowel (zero ending). There are many exceptions — this is an area to approach with drilling rather than memorisation of every rule.

examples.meaning: book → of books (gen. pl.) — zero ending
word → of words (gen. pl.) — zero ending

questions.explanation: 2, 3, 4 → genitive singular. So: три дома (three houses).
5 and above (except the teen-ending numbers 21, 31...) → genitive plural. семь домов.
11–19 always take genitive plural regardless of the last digit. 11 домов.

cards.title: много, мало, сколько + genitive plural
cards.body: The quantifying words много (many), мало (few), and сколько (how many) all require the genitive plural — same as 5+. So: много книг (many books), мало денег (little money), сколько уроков (how many lessons). Learn this alongside the number rule — it's the same underlying logic.

cards.title: Practical priority
cards.body: Cases are your declared weak point — so let's be direct. The counting rule is one of the most drilled items in Russian because it comes up in every quantity expression. Don't try to derive it in real time — drill the three zones until they're automatic: one → bare noun; two/three/four → add А; five-plus → add ОВ (for masculine). Exceptions come with exposure.

questions.explanation: один дом — '1' ends in 1: nominative singular.
три дома — '3' is in zone 2-4: genitive singular. дом → дома.
семь домов — '7' is in zone 5+: genitive plural. дом → домов.
две книги — '2' is in zone 2-4: genitive singular. книга → книги.
десять книг — '10' is in zone 5+: genitive plural. книга → книг (zero ending).
много уроков — много takes genitive plural, same as 5+. урок → уроков.
11–19 always take genitive plural. The 'ends in 1' rule does NOT apply to teens.

rules: After 5–20 and 0 (and 25, 26...): genitive plural — пять домов
11–19 always take genitive plural — the teens exception
много / мало / сколько / несколько all take genitive plural
summary.nextLesson: Next: Unit 4 — present tense verb conjugation. The two conjugation classes and the six personal endings.
